title: Airlines Tell Congress: The Data-Sharing Problem Is Bigger Than the Tech
summary: At a June 23 Senate hearing on NAS safety, industry leaders made clear that better information flow between airlines, airports, and controllers matters as much as new ATC hardware and AI systems — a message with direct implications for how flight ops technology gets evaluated and deployed.
body_markdown:
The FAA got its splashy AI contract last week when ASI was awarded the 12-year, $875M SMART and FMDS deal — a story I covered here on June 22. But what happened on Capitol Hill two days later arguably says just as much about where airline flight operations technology actually needs to go.

## What the Senate Heard on June 23

<cite index="69-2,69-3">Senator Jerry Moran led a Senate Commerce Subcommittee hearing with aviation industry leaders to examine recent near collisions and runway incursion incidents, with a particular focus on reviewing safety technology implementation, runway safety programs, and FAA modernization efforts.</cite> The immediate impetus was a string of unsettling incidents: <cite index="61-5,61-6">within nine days, a skydiving aircraft crashed near Butler, Missouri, killing all twelve aboard; a B-52 went down near Edwards Air Force Base; a Marine Corps F/A-18D crashed southeast of Seattle; and most recently, a runway incursion at Boston Logan forced a Delta flight to execute an emergency go-around after ATC cleared an American Airlines aircraft into its landing path.</cite>

But the more durable thread running through the hearing wasn't any single incident — it was data. <cite index="60-1">Airlines for America CEO Chris Sununu urged Congress to build on a $12.5 billion down payment toward ATC modernization, while witnesses said better information sharing could help identify risks earlier across airlines, airports, pilots, controllers, and other aviation stakeholders.</cite> That's a meaningful signal. The industry isn't just asking for new radar or faster fiber — it's asking for a more connected operational picture, one where anomalies surface across the whole system before they cascade into close calls.

## Why This Matters for Flight Ops Tech

<cite index="29-2,29-3">The SMART system, which uses data to analyze airline schedules, weather, airport capacity, airspace conditions, and operational constraints to predict traffic flows and identify potential conflicts, has airlines privately expressing concerns about how the FAA will decide which flights need to be moved when conflicts arise — and whether the fall 2026 deployment timeline is realistic.</cite> Those concerns didn't disappear once the contract was signed. If anything, the Senate hearing put them in sharper relief.

Having spent years on the delivery and deployment side of aviation software programs, I've seen this tension play out before: a genuinely capable system goes live before the data plumbing — the integrations, the feeds, the agreements about who owns what signal — is ready to support it. The technology works in isolation but struggles to deliver value in a live operational environment where everyone is still figuring out what to share with whom and under what conditions. The SMART deployment timeline is aggressive, and the information-sharing gaps the Senate heard about on June 23 are exactly the kind of friction that can undercut even well-designed systems.

<cite index="62-4,62-5">The hearing specifically examined the implementation of safety technologies, runway safety programs, and FAA modernization efforts, with witnesses expected to discuss opportunities for improved safety coordination between aviation stakeholders and the federal government.</cite> That framing — coordination, not just technology — is the right one. ADS-B In, SMART, FMDS, and the broader infrastructure overhaul being managed by prime integrator Peraton are all tools. But tools don't share data. Organizations do, and getting commercial airlines, the military, general aviation operators, and the FAA onto a common information framework is a governance challenge as much as a technical one.

## The Broader ATC Modernization Stack

It's worth stepping back and looking at how the pieces fit together right now. <cite index="80-2,80-3,80-4,80-5">Peraton has been appointed prime integrator for a major overhaul of the ATC system, supported by a $12.5 billion initial investment through the One Big Beautiful Bill and targeting delivery of a state-of-the-art system by the end of 2028.</cite> On top of that foundation, ASI is building the AI-powered traffic management layer with SMART and FMDS. And sitting underneath all of it are the data-sharing and information-flow gaps that the Senate hearing made visible this week.

<cite index="60-24,60-25">The June 23 hearing also addressed the significant challenges to widespread ADS-B In adoption, including the FAA's certification processes, supply chain limitations, maintenance capacity, and the varying requirements of different aircraft types</cite> — challenges that matter because ADS-B In data is, for many of these AI-driven traffic management systems, a primary input. If the sensor layer is incomplete, the intelligence built on top of it is only as good as what it can see.

What the hearing clarified is that the U.S. isn't building one modernization project. It's trying to coordinate several overlapping ones — infrastructure, AI software, sensing technology, and data governance — simultaneously and at speed. Whether that coordination actually happens at the pace the administration is projecting is the real question worth watching.

The Senate hearing was a useful reality check, not a rebuke of the direction. The direction looks right. The sequencing is where I'd want to probe further.
questions: Based on your time delivering aviation software programs, does the FAA's fall 2026 target for an initial SMART deployment seem realistic given the data-sharing gaps the Senate hearing surfaced — or is this the kind of timeline that tends to slip once real operational integration begins? (Realistic / Will slip / Too early to say)

The hearing witnesses flagged information-sharing between airlines, airports, pilots, and controllers as the priority — not just new hardware. From your experience on both the software vendor and delivery side, is that the right diagnosis, or is the industry using 'data sharing' as a proxy for a deeper interoperability problem that doesn't have a clean fix?

ADS-B In was a central topic — specifically the certification and supply chain barriers slowing adoption. As a private pilot with an instrument rating, do you see the cockpit-side rollout of ADS-B In as a technology readiness problem, a regulatory problem, or primarily an economic incentive problem for operators?
